items.question: Explain random forest in one minute.
items.answer: Train many decision trees in parallel. Each tree sees a bootstrap sample of rows and, at each split, only a random subset of features. For a new point, average tree outputs (regression) or take a majority vote (classification). Averaging decorrelated trees cuts variance.

items.question: What is bagging and how does it appear in a random forest?
items.answer: Bagging draws bootstrap samples with replacement and trains one model per sample, then aggregates predictions. In a random forest each tree is a bagged decision tree; the forest adds random feature subsets at splits to decorrelate trees further.

items.question: Why use max_features < p instead of all features at every split?
items.answer: If one feature is very predictive, every bagged tree may split on it first, keeping trees correlated. Random feature subsets force different paths, so averaging reduces variance more effectively.

items.question: How does a random forest differ from gradient boosting?
items.answer: Forests train independent trees in parallel on bootstraps and aggregate by vote or mean. Boosting trains shallow trees sequentially on pseudo-residuals with a learning rate. Forests target variance reduction; boosting targets bias reduction step by step.
